description: A hand can 'tapu' a door against the rain or fit a lid over a jar. The word moves an opening or access point into its closed state, and it can end access to a discussion in the same way. 'phae' (open) reverses that state; 'te' (CESS) ends an action without closing an object or route.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper front teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release it into stressed central 'a'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p', then release them while raising the back of the tongue and rounding the lips for high-back 'u'.
sound_symbolism: A complete dental closure and a complete lip closure briefly interrupt the two syllables before each vowel releases. Those firm contacts give the form a small shut-and-open rhythm.
usage_notes: The door, lid, container, route, or discussion being closed precedes 'tapu'. 'kirato' (lock) is the mechanism that can hold a closure, while 'kathu' (seal) is an object or mark used for closure or authentication.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A jar shut for storage and a gate closed before weather reaches the animals are ordinary acts of keeping. 'tapu' names the closing; the thing protected and the reason stay visible around it.

semantic_domains.activity: the act that brings an opening or access point into a closed state
semantic_domains.physical: a door, lid, or container moved into closure
semantic_domains.spatial: passage or access ended at a boundary
